sanskrit: देहिनोऽस्मिन्यथा देहे कौमारं यौवनं जरा । तथा देहान्तरप्राप्तिर्धीरस्तत्र न मुह्यति ॥
hindiTranslation: देहधारीके इस मनुष्यशरीरमें जैसे बालकपन, जवानी और वृद्धावस्था होती है, ऐसे ही देहान्तरकी प्राप्ति होती है ।  उस विषयमें धीर मनुष्य मोहित नहीं होता ॥
englishTranslation: Just as the embodied one passes through childhood, youth, and old age in this body, so too it passes into another body. The wise are not deluded by this.
essence: What changes form is not what you truly are.
speaker: Krishna
simpleMeaning: The body changes through stages, and the one living in it moves on to another body. A steady person does not get confused by this.
simpleInsight: There is relief in seeing change as natural, not tragic.
detailedMeaning: On the battlefield of Kurukshetra, Arjuna is frozen by grief. Krishna first reminds him that no one was truly born or lost, then gives a human example: the body moves from childhood to youth to old age, and the embodied one moves on as well.
modernRelevance: You watch an old photo and barely recognise yourself. The face, voice, and fears have changed — yet something in you has kept witnessing all of it.
translationLiteral: देहिनः (the embodied one) / अस्मिन् (in this) / यथा (just as) / देहे (in the body) / कौमारम् (childhood) / यौवनम् (youth) / जरा (old age) / तथा (so) / देहान्तरप्राप्तिः (attaining another body) / धीरः (the wise) / तत्र (there) / न (not) / मुह्यति (is deluded)
insight: It shifts attention from the body’s changing stages to the steady awareness that outlives them.

quoteVariants.text: Childhood, youth, and age pass. The embodied one passes on too.
Bodies change. The wise do not panic.
I change bodies the way I change ages.
